sentences.text: I-- I just-- my body just became numb.
My mind was like, I didn't care when I died.
Because eventually, I was gonna die.
I was only hoping to hang on long enough to die where the gas chambers wouldn't be around anymore.
Because I didn't want to become ashes.
And so with the help of God, I guess, I endured.
And the help like that nurse with other people around that, we weren't even aware that they were helping us.
And like my friend Sabka with the support of trying to explain to me and make me strong and to maybe understand a little better why to live.

And-- and I guess all this put together and remembering what I lost and remembering that they will never be and maybe I will be lucky enough to do things for them, the things that they can't do anymore.
And so with all of this put together, I think I had more luck than-- that sense.
And as a 13-year-old, maybe it was more possible for me than for a 20-year-old or 30-year-old.
I don't know.
The answer is not-- is not-- I don't have the-- the-- the right or the wrong or the-- or the answer to that.
The fact is that I know that I-- sometimes, I wished I was dead.
Sometimes, I was walking the road where I knew they were gonna kill me.
And I didn't.
And sometimes I just wanted to live.
So just depends, you know, what happened.
And-- and terrible things happened.
And so--
My friend Sabka was taken away from-- from that barrack on the next zahlappell, and I was left again in the-- in the barracks.
I don't know where she went.
I don't know where they took her.
But I didn't think that they took her to another experiment.
Because according to the record, she was dead.
She-- we then had another number.
I-- we no longer had the number in our arm, but we had a number on our clothing.
We got a-- a piece of clothing.
It was a stripe dress.
It was the blue and gray stripe dress.
And on the dress was sewn the new number.
I don't remember the new number, but I'll never forget the old number, even though I was told to forget it.
And so she-- she was taken away the next zahlappell, and remained there.
And I was just-- they were liquidate-- I don't know whether they were liquidating, but every couple of hours, there was selections and zahlappell.
And Mengele would come in and select all the people from the [NON-ENGLISH].
And then they would disappear.
And there were less and less people left in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] lager, in the [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
And then I saw things.
It was like, I think it was in August or in September.
I don't remember.
See, we-- we didn't have conception of time or day or year or holidays.
But for some reason, we knew when Yom Kippur was.
And we didn't-- we didn't eat our ration.
We were fasting.
And then we didn't get food for five days because they found out we weren't eating it.
And then I think it was in August or Tisha B'Av or one of the holidays that is like celebrated-- a happy celebration.
I don't really remember which one.
Sukkot maybe or it was-- it was one of those holidays that it's no very, very observed by everybody.
But it was a holiday.
It was a Jewish holiday.
And we had just come in from the zahlappell.
We were out there for like four hours in the rain and-- and-- and mud and everything.
And we were ordered to go out again.
And they took us out to the front of the camp.
It was so close to the crematorium that the ashes were falling on the ground where we were.
And they ordered us to sit on the-- on the ground.
We sat in the hot ashes.
And the ashes from the crematorium were coming down like-- like little-- little drizzles of-- of rain on us.
And they told us they were gonna give us a concert to celebrate the holidays.
And so 32,000 women or less, after they had taken some away, sat on the ground in the hot ashes, the-- all crematoriums, all seven of them were burning like you would never.
The flames and the ashes were unbearable.
The stench from the bodies-- it was such a gray day even though it wasn't raining anymore.
It was like night time.
It was like so dark.
And there was this platform in front of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all these beautiful women in crisp uniforms with make-up and lipstick and blonde, long hair-- we had no hair.
But they were-- they came up and these-- these SS men, young guys, strong, well-fed, well-preserved with-- with clean clothes, sipping something.
You know, either they had a cup or they had something wrapped up and they were eating, and they were laughing and talking to one another, having a great time.
And we're sitting there, motionless.
We were supposed to sit and just watch them.
And then now the band came.
The band came up on this platform-- little children in striped uniforms.
One had a violin.
One had a piccolo-- Jewish children with Jewish stars.
That was the band.
That was the concert.
And they were playing.

They ordered them to play music like-- like love songs and not-- not-- not music for our souls, like for the holidays or [INAUDIBLE] or maybe for our heritage or maybe something-- some little glimpse of hope for the future.
But you know, I-- I didn't-- I had no romance or anything in mind or I was too young to have a husband or a boyfriend.
But people sitting, not having their husbands or not having-- I was thinking of my brothers and of my parents.
And here are these people all laughing and eating.
[SOBBING] And our people are burning next door.
And the flames are-- and how could they-- how can they do this?
And they're-- they're-- they're wanting us to have a good time, listening to the band and having a good time.
And we sat there for hours and in such a torture.
And you can see everybody was like squirming and-- and-- and just wanted to die.
And you can, like, see everybody's thoughts where we are.
You know, we will never get there.
We will never be that.
We will never enjoy this band.
We will never have the clothes that they have.
We will never have the lipstick or the hair.
And it was just pure torture for hours.
And then they just let us go back to the barrack.
And-- and that was another way, I guess, of killing us.
And then we still-- I still stayed there and they weren't taking me anywhere.
They selected me one day to go to work to Kanada, which we called [INAUDIBLE].
And-- I worked there for three days.

I was sorting clothes and putting it in piles so they can take it to Germany and still watching the trains coming in with people and taking their suitcases and selecting out of the suitcases all the things.
And I worked there for three days.
And I met-- I came across-- I worked with a-- with a person from my town.
And she was working with jewelry.
And she said to me, are you in in lager [INAUDIBLE]?
She was in lager A.
And I says, yeah.
And she says, my-- my sisters are there in barrack 32.
She says, I want you to give this to them before they-- they are-- they're being taken away.
And she handed me something rolled up, really, really tiny that was no bigger than maybe the-- the-- the nail of my thumb.
And-- and she said, give that-- please give that to her.
And see, when I went to work to Kanada, there was an abundance of everything.
We unpacked things like jewelry and-- but we weren't interested in that.
We only wanted shoes and food.
So we were allowed to go in a pair of shoes and come back.
But what-- what we did is we would go in a pair of wooden shoes.
We would take from one of the inmates and then leave it in the mud and put on a pair of good shoes and come back to the camp and give it to somebody that had no shoes.
And then we would again take a pair of wooden shoes.
Because the wooden shoes didn't last.
Mine only lasted two days.
I stepped in the mud and I stepped out and they remained in the mud.
And I-- I couldn't go get them.
They-- they would kill me.
So this is what we did it.
Because shoes and-- and, like, aspirin if we found aspirin or something-- something that wouldn't hurt anybody.
Like, but if you're-- everybody had diarrhea.
Everybody was dying from diarrhea.
Once you had diarrhea, you were gone.
So that's what we were trying to bring home.
Sometimes, they caught us.
Because-- but most of the times, they didn't examine us.
And they did allow us to wear the shoes.
We were allowed to take shoes-- like, exchange our own shoes.
So they didn't pay attention to our shoes.
But they, you know, like-- and again, they were the people that were in charge.
And they would tell us, don't take anything back today because they're going to search.
So-- And these were Jews that told you?
Oh yeah, yeah.
These were Jews.
But they were in charge of the groups, you know, that were assigned to certain jobs.
And one day, one girl in my group didn't listen and she-- and it was a very-- see, when the-- when the transport was really an abundance of things, that's what they search.
And they have all different things coming in that they knew that the survivors would-- the inmates would-- would take.
Then they searched.
But of course, we weren't-- we weren't paying attention to that.
We were looking for survival.
And most of us listened to the person that told us not to do on that way.
And the reason that she-- that she knew it is because she was friends with the SS man that guarded us.
She was from Germany, and she went to school with him.
And so he-- he was-- he was, you know, still being some sort of a friend to her.
So that's how she knew it.
And so she told us, don't take anything back today.
And this person that didn't listen to her, she happened to be very fond of her.
She was also very young.
In fact, I think she was even younger than me.
And she-- we had a transport that had so much food and so much good stuff that everybody in camp needed it.
And she just-- the way we did it is we lined our bodies with everything and put our clothes over it.
And that's how we brought it into camp.
But if they didn't search us, we were lucky.
If they searched us, we were dead.
So she didn't listen.
And she packed her body up with food.
And before-- and she just had a feeling that she shouldn't let her go through the gate.
So she came up to her.
And she says, if you have any food, get rid of it because you're gonna to be searched.
And she denied that she has food.
So she didn't listen.
But her friend that told her things, he came and he searched us before we went to the gate.
And he caught her.
And this-- again, see how this Jewish girl came up.
And she started to beat her up-- punch her and beat her up, punishing her for taking the food.
So he let go.
See, he wasn't-- because she-- she did the job that he was supposed to do and then shoot her.
But he went away and left it up to her that she's going to punish her.
Again, she saved somebody's life.
So we-- we-- from then on in, everybody listened to her.
And that's how-- so-- but I only worked there for three days.
But things-- you know, you-- we-- I saw in the three days-- because you were so close to the trains again.
And you saw the people coming off the train with the children and the suitcases.
And you already knew what happened to you.
You already knew what's going to happen to them.
Only it was lesser and lesser things that they took-- took-- to-- into the camps.
They went straight to the crematorium.
Some of the trains came in, they never even selected.
They just packed them on the trucks, and they went straight to the crematorium-- young and old and-- and-- didn't matter.
They just-- they just had to get it done as fast as they could.
And then-- and then after I worked there for three days and then they weren't calling us anymore because they had enough people from the other-- that were assigned to that.
We were just fill-ins, like, that they took from our camp to work there for a little while.
And then the camp became very restless because they started to, like, taking too many people too fast.
So people were hiding during zahlappell, not to be taken away.
And then zahlappells lasted longer because they had to find the people that-- that didn't show up to zahlappell.
They had to account for everybody.
So people were dying at zahlappell because it was too long and too cold and too-- they were too sick.
And it was-- something was happening.
We-- we didn't know what.
But they were just too much of a hurry.
And-- it's-- then we found out it's because the-- the war was, like, kind of coming near us, closer.
When do you think this was?
Is this '45 already?
This was-- no, no, still in '44.
This was like around September-- August, September, October.
Because I did not leave Birkenau until-- I think it was in December.
In January, they liquidated everything.
Because January 5, I think Auschwitz was liberated.
And we were like the last-- I was saved by a Sonderkommando boy.
He-- he worked in the Sonderkommando.
And they selected me with the gypsies.
They took me out with the gypsies.
See, they-- they were already evacuating.
And some of the people were being taken to Germany to-- to labor camps and some of them to the gas chamber.
So it just depends how strong you were or how healthy you were.
The selections were very rapid.
Every couple of hours, there were selections.
And--
And I was still in C.
And then I think it was in December.
Because it was cold, the snow-- it was really very bad.
And the gypsy camp was not too far from the C lager.
It was, like, in the corner.
But there was-- it was a family gypsy camp.
It was, like, the families were together.
And I was just taken out of my-- my-- zahlappell, all by myself, all single.
The-- I think that was Mengele that took me out of there.
And he said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
What are you doing here?
Like, you don't belong here.
You should have been gone long ago.
Don't forget, I was 13.
I was maybe four feet tall.
And I was in there for how many months already?
I must've been skin and bone, looking that I should have been in the gas chamber a long time ago according to his statistic.
And here I am.

But see, every selection after I came back from the experiment [INAUDIBLE] the number, I would-- every zahlappell, I would stand in the middle row-- like, in between people so I shouldn't be noted-- noticed.
Because everybody was taller and older.
And I was this little thing.
Like, I was always afraid.
So I-- and people from the barrack always took me in the middle of them and stood around me so I shouldn't be seen from the outside.
But somehow, he spotted me.
And he went through the zahlappell.
And he ordered the block elders to-- to take me out, whatever.
He told them to take me out.
And she did.
She-- when everybody went inside, I stood out there and she came out and she took me to the gate.
And they opened the gate and I went out.
And there was a transport of gypsies coming through.
And she just attached me to that gypsy person, and I went to the gas chamber.
And as I was going into the gas chamber-- of course, we didn't know where we were going.
And I figure, oh, good.
This is families.
You know, we'll be safe because families don't go together to the gas chamber.
I never-- you know, my parents didn't go together.
This is men and women and children.
So I thought I was safe.
You know, we're going to-- to work somewhere, to a family camp, to another family camp.
And-- but when we got to the gas chamber, it was too late.
I mean, we were there.
But the gas chamber-- the gas chamber was the number three in Birkenau.
And as everybody-- you know, everybody was being pushed into the gas-- to the gas chamber.
And I was going backwards.
Somehow, I was being pushed backwards.
And I got stuck in the door.
Because as you were walking down the gas chamber, the-- the passage became narrower and narrower and narrower.
And when you came to the door, it was very narrow.
And I was like stuck in the door.
And I-- I don't know.
I guess God told me to do that.
I hung onto the-- to the edge of the door and I just stood there, hanging on for dear life.
And everybody was like past-- past me.
And-- and everybody was in, and I was at the door.
And everybody was up to [INAUDIBLE].
It was so jam packed.
And I still didn't know what they were gonna do there.
But I knew it was a crematorium because I saw the chimney.
But I didn't know the procedure.
So I just hung onto the door.
Well, one of the SS men came to close the door so they can throw the Zyklon B in.
Because they were all standing around looking in somewhere, you know, up by-- see, it's close-- it was close to the ground.
And the door was like underground.
So he came to close the door.
And they were yelling to hurry up to close the door.
And he couldn't close the door because it was so full, and I was like stuck in the door.
So instead of-- and I was naked.
I had no clothes.
So that's when they took our clothes away.
I knew we were going to the gas chamber.
And he couldn't push me in because it was so full.
And they were yelling at him.
And he was afraid, I guess.
He was only a guard.
So he grabbed me and he threw me out.
And he closed the door.
This was an SS.
And he-- and I took the-- I mean, I ran.
I ran up the ramp.
And I was naked.
I didn't know where to go, what to do.
It was nothing inside except barbed wire and snow.
And I couldn't go back.
So-- it happened so fast.
I went under the eve of the crematorium.
And I laid under the eve, and I hid there.
What made me do it, I don't know.
Why did I ever think that I was going to survive it?
I don't know, but I did it.
And I laid there.
And I heard all these screams and all these yells and all these beggings.
And-- all-- and it took a few minutes.
And then it was quiet.
It was so quiet.
Not-- you can hear a pin drop.
And then I said, oh my God.
They-- they-- they went away.
Everybody went away.
Nobody's here.
And then I saw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young boys coming, you know, walking with Jewish stars on them.
And one boy came to open that door that that SS man closed.
And he-- he found me.
He saw me underneath the-- and he came up to me.
And he pulled part of me out.
And he-- he says-- he took his jacket off.
He says, put this on and go back there.
And when I finish, I will be back.
I know who you are.
And see how he knew who I was?
In lager C, one day they gave me a job to be a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] means a gofer And I was picking up papers from the SS, taking them down the-- the road to be signed for zahlappell for the block elders [INAUDIBLE] and take them back.
After that, I was standing guard by the gate to let work men in.

After the SS let them in, I was to report whoever was coming in like electrician or a plumber or somebody that delivered something to report them to the SS, sign them in for how much time and all that, and then-- you know, so-- and I did that one day.
And this boy came through.
And I never saw-- the whole day, all I saw people coming through is like-- not very many came.
One-- one man came and was carrying a package with a green insignia.
Another one came with a purple one.
These were all PLOs.
Like, you know, they were in the camp.
They were prisoners of war for different reasons-- political.
And so-- and he was the only one with the Jewish star.
And I said, oh my God.
A Jew-- I haven't seen a Jewish boy around for so long.
And but he came up to me and he told me what he was doing, he says, I'm a plumber and I will be here.
I'm supposed to be here for an hour to fix something by the SS office, so, you know, quarters.
And he says-- and he saw my-- he knew I was Jewish.
And he says, I work in the-- real fast-- I work in the Sonderkommando.
And one of my sisters, my only sister that's left alive in this camp, gave me extra time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I says, I don't want to see your face.
I looked at him, but I didn't want to remember his face.
Because if he gets caught, I didn't want to identify him, you know?
And I says, you got it.
You got an extra hour.
And I signed him in for later so he can go see his sister.
And he was working in the Sonderkommando.
And when I was underneath the eve, he came to open the door and he found me.
I didn't recognize him, but he knew-- he recognized me.
And he said, I'll be right back.
When I'm finished, I'll be back.
And he took off his jacket and he gave me his jacket to put on.
And within five minutes, he was back.
Because everybody-- it doesn't take long for a crew to exterminate God know show many hundreds of gypsies and-- and bodies, you know?
And he was back-- back very short time.
He says, now listen.
Soon as they turn off the electricity and the wires, there is a train going to a labor camp.
And it's going to come in between the two camps.
They're going to put 50 people in each barrack or whatever.
If I find an open cart, I will throw you over the wires into the cart.
When you get-- if you make it into the cart, don't make yourself obvious.
Do not stay in the front and do not stay in the back.
Be part of whatever's going on there.
And I said, yeah, right, you know?
I mean, it was so bizarre to me.
I mean, how could you ever get out of this hell?
And he says, you're going to go to a labor camp.
And I said, OK.
But I knew.
My heart, I knew that he wasn't-- he wasn't going to be able to do that.
He has to risk his life.
But he was-- he didn't care because his life was only three months away if it was that.
So-- and he did come.
And he did take me.
And he brought a blanket and he rolled me up in this blanket.
And I was only 60 pounds to begin with.
And he threw me over those wires.
And I landed in an open wagon on that train.
And the train took off and the people that were on that train never even uttered a word who I was, why I was thrown in there.
They I guess just knew that somebody saved my life.
And we-- this train went to this camp deep into to a small labor camp that had a factory.
And they told us that we are going to fix the machines that make potato chips.
Of course, we never saw potato chips.
It could've been ammunition, and we were told there was potato chips.
We never saw any.
But the people that were on that train was mechanics, machine mechanics.
And I was supposed to be that.
I didn't even know what to do with a hammer, no less being a mechanic.
But we did get into this camp.
It was a very small camp.
We-- I thought I was in a hotel.
It was-- I have it on a map, but I can never remember the town.
It in-- in Sudetendeutschland somewhere's.
And we went in there at night of course so we shouldn't be noticed by people that live there.
And we are assigned to a bunk.
Each one had their separate bunks-- like a single bed with straw in it and a pillow and a blanket.
And I said, no, this is a mistake.
This is not for us.
This can't be.
We can't be so lucky.
And of course, there was only 50 of us in-- in-- that I know.
I mean, the train was long.
I don't know whether they all-- they couldn't have all wound up there because the camp wasn't that big.
It was one building.
